note: Security.txt serves a standards-compliant /.well-known/security.txt (security.txt draft RFC / RFC 9116) from one settings form. All state is the single config object securitytxt.settings; a SecuritytxtController + SecuritytxtSerializer render it as text/plain. The file is only served when securitytxt.settings:enabled is TRUE (otherwise the /.well-known/* routes throw NotFoundHttpException -> 404), and viewing is gated by the 'view securitytxt' permission while editing needs 'administer securitytxt'. Configure route is securitytxt.configure at /admin/config/system/securitytxt. There are no plugins, entities, hooks-for-others, or Drush commands, so no plugin/hook/drush tier. All tiers are grounded in securitytxt.settings and the served file. Hard verify scripts assert live config (configure-verify) and the actual serialized file body (serve-file-verify), not curl'd headers, so grading does not depend on the anonymous 'view securitytxt' grant.

evals.prompt: On a Drupal 11 site using the Security.txt module, at what URL path is the security.txt file served, and what content type does it use?
evals.expected_output: It is served at /.well-known/security.txt (route securitytxt.securitytxt_file) as text/plain; the detached signature is at /.well-known/security.txt.sig. The routes 404 unless securitytxt.settings:enabled is TRUE.

evals.prompt: Where do I configure the Security.txt module, and which config object and keys hold the contact, expiry date and encryption-key values?
evals.expected_output: Configure at Admin -> Configuration -> System -> Security.txt (route securitytxt.configure, path /admin/config/system/securitytxt). All values live in the securitytxt.settings config object, including contact_email/contact_phone/contact_page_url, expiry_date (a Unix timestamp), and encryption_key_url.

evals.prompt: The Security.txt module defines its own permissions. Which permission lets anonymous users actually read /.well-known/security.txt, and which permission is needed to edit the disclosure details?
evals.expected_output: Reading /.well-known/security.txt (and .sig) requires the 'view securitytxt' permission, which you grant to Anonymous and Authenticated. Editing the config/sign forms requires 'administer securitytxt' (restrict access: TRUE).

evals.prompt: This site already has Security.txt configured. What contact email address is currently published in the security.txt file? Inspect the live configuration to answer.
evals.expected_output: contact_email is security@known-example.com, readable with `drush config:get securitytxt.settings contact_email`. It is emitted as the 'Contact: mailto:security@known-example.com' line.

evals.prompt: Security.txt is configured on this site. The expiry_date is stored as a Unix timestamp in securitytxt.settings. What is that timestamp value currently set to? Inspect the live configuration.
evals.expected_output: expiry_date is 1893456000 (2030-01-01 UTC). Read it with `drush config:get securitytxt.settings expiry_date`; it renders in the file as the ISO-8601 'Expires:' line.

evals.prompt: This site's Security.txt is enabled. Which security policy URL is currently published, and which preferred languages are advertised? Inspect the live configuration.
evals.expected_output: policy_url is https://known-example.com/security-policy (the 'Policy:' line) and preferred_languages is 'en, nl' (the 'Preferred-Languages:' line). Read via `drush config:get securitytxt.settings`.

evals.prompt: Configure and enable the Security.txt module on this Drupal site: in securitytxt.settings set enabled TRUE, a contact email of security@example.com, an expiry_date (a Unix timestamp) in the future, and a policy_url of https://example.com/security-policy. Clear caches when done.
evals.expected_output: securitytxt.settings has enabled = TRUE, contact_email containing an @, expiry_date > 0, and policy_url starting with https://, so /.well-known/security.txt serves Contact/Expires/Policy lines.

evals.prompt: Make the Security.txt module actually serve a valid security.txt file: enable it in securitytxt.settings with a contact email (security@example.com) and an encryption key URL (https://example.com/pgp-key.txt), then clear caches so the file at /.well-known/security.txt renders both a 'Contact: mailto:' and an 'Encryption:' line.
evals.expected_output: securitytxt.settings is enabled with contact_email security@example.com and encryption_key_url https://example.com/pgp-key.txt, so the securitytxt.serializer renders a body containing 'Contact: mailto:security@example.com' and 'Encryption: https://example.com/pgp-key.txt'.
